Where Satan cannot persuade people to view the judgment to come as uncertain, he achieves his goal by persuading them to view it as distant. These rulers dare to say, “We are as safe in this city as flesh in a boiling pot; the walls of the city will be to us as walls of brass, and we will receive no more damage from the besiegers than the caldron does from the fire.” When sinners flatter themselves toward their own ruin, it is time to tell them they will have no peace if they continue. None will remain in possession of the city but those who are buried in it. Those who feel most secure are least safe. God is often pleased to single out some sinners for warning to others. Whether Pelatiah died at that time in Jerusalem, or when the fulfillment of the prophecy drew near, is uncertain. Like Ezekiel, we ought to be deeply affected by the sudden death of others, and we should still plead with the Lord to have mercy on those who remain.

The faithful captives in Babylon were insulted by the Jews who remained in Jerusalem; but God made gracious promises to them. It is promised that God will give them one heart—a heart firmly fixed for God and not wavering. All who are made holy have a new spirit, a new temper, and dispositions; they act from new principles, walk by new rules, and aim at new ends. A new name or a new face will not be enough without a new spirit. If anyone is in Christ, he is a new creature. The carnal heart, like a stone, cannot be made to feel. People live among the dead and dying and are neither concerned nor humbled. He will make their hearts tender and fit to receive impressions; this is God's work, it is his gift by promise; and a wonderful and happy change is brought about by it, from death to life. Their practices will be agreeable to those principles. These two must and will go together. When the sinner feels his need of these blessings, let him present the promises as prayers in the name of Christ; they will be fulfilled.

Here is God’s departure from the city and temple. The vision went up from the Mount of Olives, which represents Christ's ascension to heaven from that very mountain. Though the Lord will not abandon his people, he may be driven away from any part of his visible church by their sins, and they will suffer when He withdraws his presence, glory, and protection.
